title: Using Databases for Legal Research — Companion Notes
subtitle: Ch. 2 · Introduction to Legal Research Platforms
intro: Two situations cover almost all database work: you have a citation and retrieve, or you don't and you search. Record what you learn about each so the difference becomes reflex.

fields.label: Case citation — which parts are the address, and which are only descriptive?
Statute citation — what does each piece (title, code, section) tell me?
Regulation citation — how does it differ from the statute's address?
What do I actually type to pull up a source — and why can typing more interfere?
Natural language — how does the database decide what I see, and what does that mean for what I might miss?
Terms and connectors (Boolean) — what do I control that natural language does not?
When in my research would I start natural, and what tells me it is time to get precise?

sections.heading: The connectors, in my own words

fields.label: AND / OR / NOT — what does each do to my result set?
"Quoted phrase" — when does word order matter?
The root expander (!) and wildcard (*) — what variations do they capture?

footer: A search is an argument to the database. Know what you asked for, so you know what you got.
